nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; typically found over coral and rocky bottoms along the Brazilian mainland coast and at offshore islands, generally at depths of about 3–20 m. Often observed in small groups operating cleaning stations.

diet.notes: Carnivorous; generally accepts a varied diet of meaty frozen foods such as mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, cyclops, and finely chopped seafood, alongside high-quality marine pellets and flakes. In the wild it has been documented picking ectoparasites from larger fish at cleaning stations; in captivity it tends to adapt readily to prepared foods even when no host fish are present. Multiple small feedings per day are commonly advised.

avoidWith: aggressive fish that may outcompete for food

summary: A small Brazilian cleaner goby with a black body and bright yellow lateral stripe, generally considered beginner-friendly and reef-safe. Widely available as captive-bred stock.
careNotes: The Yellowline Goby (Elacatinus figaro), also commonly sold as the Barber Goby, is endemic to the southwest Atlantic off Brazil where it operates cleaning stations on reefs, picking ectoparasites from larger fish including blennies, damselfishes, and groupers. The species was formally described by Sazima, Moura & Rosa in 1997 and is listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List, largely due to aquarium-trade collection pressure on wild populations; captive-bred specimens (notably from ORA) are widely available and are generally a more sustainable choice. It is small (commonly reported around 3–4 cm; FishBase gives 3.4 cm SL for males and 2.5 cm SL for females, while some retailer sources cite up to about 5 cm), peaceful toward other species, and generally reef-safe with corals and invertebrates. Sources tend to agree on a minimum tank size of about 38 L (10 US gal) for a single specimen, but a 75 L (≈20 US gal) or larger reef provides better long-term stability and room for a pair or small group; conspecifics may show mild territoriality in tighter quarters, so groups are best introduced simultaneously to a system with ample rockwork. Like most gobies it has been known to jump, so a tightly-fitting lid is generally advised. Lifespan in captivity has been reported at roughly 1–2 years, similar to congeneric Elacatinus cleaner gobies. The Yellowline Goby has been documented to hybridize with the Neon Goby (Elacatinus oceanops) under aquarium conditions, producing offspring marketed as 'Hybrid Cleaner Goby'. Note that the trade name 'Neon Gold Stripe Goby' or 'Gold Neon Goby' is more commonly associated with the closely related Caribbean Elacatinus evelynae — purchasers should confirm the scientific name when sourcing.
breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity, including on a commercial scale (e.g., ORA). Pairs form and lay adhesive demersal eggs on hard substrates; eggs have been reported to hatch in roughly a week, after which the pelagic larvae generally require rotifers and small zooplankton before graduating to larger foods. Larval rearing is considered achievable but delicate. Yellowline Gobies can also interbreed with Neon Gobies (Elacatinus oceanops) under aquarium conditions, producing fertile-looking hybrids.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: LiveAquaria collection page did not host a dedicated Yellowline Goby (Elacatinus figaro) listing at access time; general goby husbandry guidance (tight lid required, ~3-inch typical adult size, rockwork and coral sand recommended) was extracted from the collection page and corroborated against species-specific sources below.

additional.name: orafarm.com
additional.notes: Captive-breeder source. Confirmed scientific name Elacatinus figaro, peaceful cleaner behavior, acceptance of frozen and dry foods, and documented hybridization with Neon Goby.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name (Sazima, Moura & Rosa, 1997), taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes), Brazilian endemic distribution (7°S–28°S), reef habitat at 3–20 m, max size 3.4 cm SL (male) / 2.5 cm SL (female), and IUCN Vulnerable status (2022).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry: 10 gal (~38 L) minimum, temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, peaceful temperament, easy care, reef-safe. Adult size cited as ~5 cm (slightly above FishBase wild-data max).

additional.name: That Pet Place
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 10 gal (~38 L) minimum, temperature 75–82°F (24–28°C), pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.020–1.024, carnivorous diet (cyclops, baby brine, mysis), peaceful toward heterospecifics with moderate conspecific territoriality, captive-bred Brazilian origin. Adult size cited as 1.5 in (~3.8 cm).

fish.breedingNotes: Bred commercially in captivity. Pairs form readily and lay adhesive demersal eggs on hard substrates; eggs hatch in roughly a week and larvae require rotifers and copepods before graduating to larger foods. Larval rearing is achievable but delicate.
